text: The left hand grasper is used to grab the fatty tissue, creating tension to facilitate dissection around the vessel wall. The surgeon carefully avoids cutting too close to the vessel to prevent injury.

The screen displays instructions to dissect through the fat layer to expose the vessel wall. Residual fatty tissue remains on the anterior surface of the vessel, preventing progression to the next exercise step.

The surgeon uses scissors to scrape off residual fatty tissue from the vessel without applying energy, revealing the vessel wall. With the vessel now well exposed, the next step involves bipolar coagulation.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the vessel at two indicated points and performs bipolar coagulation until the vessel turns white, indicating satisfactory coagulation. The next step involves cutting the vessel in the middle of the coagulated sections, while avoiding excessive tension or injury to the vessel.
